title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: LCFS Credit Prices Rise; Volumes Witness a Drop WoW
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights

(8thâ€“ 14th December 2025)
  • A total of 421,420 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a fall of 21.78% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed an increase of 1.72% WoW as 1,511,597 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $51.43/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 1.82% rise WoW; prices of all non-zero transfers saw a rise of 7.94%, priced at $58.02/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price rose by 4.15% WoW; on 16th December, the spot price stood at $55.00/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $55.13/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices fell by 0.83% WoW. Both D4 and D5 RIN prices rose by 0.98% WoW; D6 RIN prices fell by 0.96% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

Last week, the California Air Resources Board (CARB) issued its multimedia evaluation of gasoline containing up to 15% ethanol, prepared jointly with the EPA and the Office of the State Fire Marshal. The analysis assesses potential impacts on public health and the environment, focusing on air, water, and soil. This release fulfills a requirement of AB 30, which allows the interim sale of E15 blends in California while regulatory review is ongoing. CARB has also published an FAQ to guide stakeholders on AB 30 implementation and existing fuel regulations.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_609251" align="alignnone" width="828"] Table 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 transfer prices recorded a 1.82% WoW increase, rising from $50.51 to $51.43 this week, and all non-zero transfer prices rose from $53.75 to $58.02, recording a rise of 7.94%. Type 1 trades witnessed 421,420 credits exchanged, recording a 21.78% fall WoW. The total transaction volumes for all non-zero trades saw an increase of 1.72% WoW, reaching 1,511,597 credits. [caption id="attachment_609252" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_609253" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices rose by 4.15% WoW, reaching $53.50/MT. On 16th December, the spot price stood at $55.00/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $55.13/MT on 16th December.

LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_609254" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 75 trades were reported in the market, with 16 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $79.25 for a Type 2 transaction at a volume of 10,000 credits. The lowest transfer by price was $45.75 per credit for a Type 2 transaction reported for 10,000 credits. The largest volume of transfers involved 300,000 credits for a Type 2 transaction, priced at $76.50. The smallest volume of transfers involved 598 credits for a Type 1 transaction priced at $51.00.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_609255" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average spot Type 1 price stood at $50.91 in December so far, falling from $52.83 in November. As of 13th December 2025, trade volumes for the month reached 3.00M.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_609256" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 8th â€“ 14th December 2025, total VPT rose by 10.95% WoW, with a rise in all non-zero credit prices for the last week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices fell by 0.83% WoW. Both D4 and D5 RIN prices rose by 0.98% WoW; D6 RIN prices fell by 0.96% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming DatesÂ 

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments This Week

  • California Multimedia Evaluation of Gasoline Containing up to 15 percent Ethanol (E15) with Staff Written Summary: On December 11, 2025, the California Air Resources Board (CARB) released the California Multimedia Evaluation of Gasoline Containing up to 15 percent Ethanol (E15), accompanied by a Staff Written Summary. Prepared by an interagency team including CalEPA and the Office of the State Fire Marshal, this extensive evaluation ensures that introducing higher ethanol blends will not adversely affect public health or the environment, specifically analyzing impacts on air, water, and soil. This publication is a key requirement under Assembly Bill (AB) 30, which immediately authorizes the sale of gasoline blends containing 10.5% to 15% ethanol in California. These sales are permitted until the California Environmental Policy Committee completes its review of the evaluation and CARB either adopts final E15 regulations or determines specific requirements cannot be met. To assist stakeholders, CARB has also posted an FAQ document regarding AB 30 implementation and current reformulated gasoline regulations.
  • California Invests Over $835 Million in Green Transportation Projects to Drive Emissions Reduction through Cap-and-Invest Program: Governor Gavin Newsom today announced more than $865 million in new investments â€” funded entirely by big polluters â€” to build affordable housing, expand transit, and protect communities from climate change. The awards include funding for 39 new affordable housing in 21 communities across the state, thousands of new homes, and major sustainable transportation upgrades, with $185.6 million going to Los Angeles County as it rebuilds infrastructure after this yearâ€™s devastating wildfires.
  • Fuel Retailers, Biodiesel Producers and Home Heating Industry Support Biodiesel Blenders' Tax Credit Legislation: America's Leading Fuel Marketers, the National Association of Convenience Stores (NACS), the National Energy & Fuels Institute (NEFI) and Sustainable Advanced Biofuel Refiners (SABR) applauded Senator Marsha Blackburn (R-Tenn.) for introducing the "Consumer Relief and Opportunities for Producers Act" (S. 3297) that would temporarily reinstate the $1 per gallon '40A' Biodiesel Blenders' Tax Credit.
  • The Clean Truck and Bus Voucher Incentive Project (HVIP) Reopens with $125 Million: The California Air Resources Board (CARB) is reopening the Clean Truck and Bus Voucher Incentive Project (HVIP) on December 16, 2025. Starting at 9:00 a.m. HVIP will begin accepting voucher request applications on a first-come first-served basis. Standard HVIP funding is currently fully subscribed, but CARB will reopen the project using funds from the recent Hino settlement and funding from cancelled vouchers. Approximately $95 million will be directed to Standard HVIP ($5 million of these funds will be in a set-aside for public fleets and tribal governments), $25 million to the Public Transit Bus Set-Aside, and $5 million to ISEF. This funding will help support zero-emission market momentum, meet ongoing fleet demand, and support small business participation. In addition, vehicle eligibility will be expanded to include Class 2b pickup trucks for vouchers requested by state or local government agencies and tribal governments.
